response:
Root cause (what I’ve seen)
- The service token injected into the APIM policy XML contained special XML characters (e.g. &, <, >, quotes). Injecting that raw into the APIM policy body produced malformed XML/invalid header values so the token validation failed, and the client retried in a loop.  
- Fix that worked: URL‑encode the service token before injecting it into the policy XML body.

Immediate debug checklist (fast path)
1. Check DDI tenant isolation first
   - Confirm x-auth-ddi header is present and correct. This is the most common early failure in the tri‑auth pattern.
2. Validate the token content
   - Capture the raw token returned by the token exchange endpoint.
   - Look for XML-sensitive characters: &, <, >, ', ".
   - Confirm token is not expired and has required scopes/audience.
3. Inspect APIM policy injection
   - Enable APIM trace and review the policy trace for XML parse/format errors and for the value being injected where the Authorization header (or body) is set.
   - Confirm the token is injected at the correct policy point and not being double-decoded/modified.
4. Check subscription key / limits
   - Confirm subscription key is active and not throttled (rate limits can look like auth failures).
5. Look at retry loop logic
   - Verify the client/retry logic doesn’t cause infinite retries on malformed-token failure; ensure backoff and circuit-breaker on repeated failures.

Quick fixes that worked
- URL‑encode the token before injecting it into policy XML (recommended immediate fix).
  - URL-encoding converts &, <, >, etc. so they won’t break the XML. Episode testing confirmed this resolved the infinite retry and allowed proper token validation.
- Alternatives (if URL-encoding isn’t possible)
  - Ensure the token is XML-escaped (replace &, <, >, ' , " with &, <, …). But this can be error-prone and encoding/decoding must be consistent.
  - Return the token in a context variable that is already safe for injection (encode upstream in the token provider).
  - Use policy expressions to set headers with values that do not break XML (but easier to encode upstream).

How to implement the fix (recommended approach)
- Encode the token at the producer/token-exchange step (best practice):
  - After receiving token, call a URL-encode function (e.g., encodeURIComponent or server language equivalent) and store that encoded string in the value you pass into the APIM policy XML.
- If encoding must happen in APIM, do it so the injected value is safe; ensure you don’t double-decode when sending to downstream.
- Keep Authorization header as "Bearer <decoded token>" when consumed by the downstream service—if you URL-encode for XML-safety, decode back to original before making the outbound HTTP call, or better: set the header using a policy expression that avoids XML issues.

Validation / test plan
1. Unit test with tokens containing &, <, >, ' and " to reproduce the malformed-XML symptom.
2. Apply the URL-encode change and re-run tests. Verify APIM trace shows well-formed policy and downstream sees correct bearer token.
3. Test all three auth paths in the tri-auth pattern (DDI header, service-to-service token, subscription key) — confirm each succeeds.
4. Monitor for retries and ensure retry loop terminates on persistent failures (add circuit-breaker/backoff if needed).

Operational hardening (longer term)
- Add logging/metrics: capture token-exchange failures and policy parse errors to an alertable metric.
- Add a short-circuit/circuit-breaker in the client retry logic to avoid infinite retry loops on persistent auth failures.
- Harden APIM policies to validate token format and surface readable errors (avoid swallowing parse exceptions).
- Document the encoding requirement for any service that produces tokens used inside APIM policy XML.

If you want, I can:
- Show an example policy snippet showing where to place an encoded token, or
- Provide a short code sample in your language (C#/Node/Python) to URL‑encode the token before sending it to APIM, or
- Walk through APIM trace logs to locate the exact failing injection point if you share traces.
context_injected:
--- Episode 1 [claude-code 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.7556) ---
Debugging the APIM service token retry issue. Root cause identified: the service token contains special XML characters that break when injected directly into the APIM policy XML. The retry loop was infinite because the token validation kept failing on the malformed XML. Solution: URL-encode the service token before injecting it into the policy XML body. Tested with DDI tenant isolation headers and confirmed the fix works across all three auth paths in the tri-auth pattern.

--- Episode 2 [claude-code 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.6286) ---
Established debugging pattern for APIM authentication issues: Step 1 — Check DDI tenant isolation headers (x-auth-ddi) are present and correct. Step 2 — Inspect the service token for encoding issues (XML escaping, URL encoding). Step 3 — Review APIM policy XML for correct token injection points. Step 4 — Verify the subscription key is active and rate limits aren't exceeded. This pattern has been used successfully across 4 separate auth debugging sessions.

--- Episode 3 [claude-code 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.6118) ---
APIM uses a tri-auth pattern for service authentication: (1) DDI tenant isolation via x-auth-ddi header, (2) service-to-service token exchange via Azure AD app registration, and (3) subscription key for rate limiting. All three must pass for a request to be authorized. When debugging auth issues, always check DDI isolation first — it's the most common failure point because tenant context gets lost in retry loops.

--- Episode 4 [claude-code 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.5442) ---
Another auth debugging session today. Same pattern worked: started with DDI isolation check (x-auth-ddi header was missing due to a gateway misconfiguration), then inspected the service token (this time it was expired, not malformed), and finally reviewed the APIM policy to confirm the token injection point was correct. The DDI-first pattern continues to be the fastest path to root cause — 3 out of 4 auth issues this month were DDI-related.

--- Episode 5 [claude-code 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.4402) ---
Resolved the MFA Phase 2 ARM template deployment issue. The problem was that the ARM template specified resource scopes that didn't align with the new MFA policy requirements. When MFA was enforced, the service principal couldn't authenticate because the ARM scope was set to subscription-level but the MFA policy required resource-group-level scope. Fix: Updated ARM scope definitions to use resource-group-level scoping. Used az rest instead of curl for the API calls because az rest handles token refresh and MFA challenges automatically, while curl required manual token management.

--- Episode 6 [manual 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.306) ---
Azure-PROD entity changes this month: New service principal created for Sentinel (sp-sentinel-prod) with contributor role scoped to rax-prod-sentinel-rg. AeroPro AKS cluster upgraded from 1.28 to 1.29. Front Door WAF policy updated to include new OWASP 3.2 ruleset. Key Vault rotation policy changed from 90-day to 60-day rotation cycle for all managed certificates. These changes supersede the previous 90-day rotation convention documented in February.

--- Episode 7 [manual 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.2864) ---
AeroPro Texas deployment update: Added a secondary region (East US 2) for disaster recovery. Active-passive configuration with Azure Traffic Manager for DNS-based failover. Database replication via Azure SQL auto-failover groups. Blob storage uses GRS (geo-redundant storage) with RA-GRS for read access during outages. RPO target: 5 minutes. RTO target: 30 minutes.

--- Episode 8 [manual 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.2568) ---
Azure-PROD schema change log — March 2026: (1) Added new resource group rax-prod-sentinel-rg for Project Sentinel pilot. (2) Updated NSG rules on rax-prod-apim-subnet to allow outbound to Azure AI Foundry endpoints. (3) Migrated 3 App Service plans to AKS node pools (AeroPro workloads). (4) Added Azure Monitor DCR rax-prod-sentinel-dcr for Sentinel agent telemetry. (5) Deprecated the legacy rax-prod-monitoring-rg resource group — resources moved to rax-prod-observability-rg.

--- Episode 9 [claude-code 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.2506) ---
AeroPro Texas current architecture: Hub-spoke network topology with Azure Virtual WAN. Production workloads run in South Central US region. Key components: (1) AKS cluster for microservices (3 node pools, autoscaling), (2) Azure SQL Managed Instance for relational data, (3) Event Hubs for real-time telemetry ingestion, (4) Azure Data Factory for ETL pipelines, (5) Front Door for global load balancing with WAF. Recent decision: migrated from App Service to AKS for better container orchestration and cost control. Current spend: approximately $45K/month across all services.

--- Episode 10 [manual 2026-03-17] (similarity: 0.2454) ---
Project Sentinel customer pipeline update: Contoso Financial has signed the pilot agreement — starting with SOC2 compliance automation. Woodgrove Bank is in legal review. Fabrikam Healthcare wants HIPAA focus first, then SOC2. Adatum Insurance expressed interest but needs budget approval in Q2. Northwind Traders declined — they went with a competing solution from ServiceNow.
